topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. A formal notification, detailing the publication of my piece on urban planning, but the author listed at the top was 'Elias Vance.' My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. I reread the attribution line three times, the words blurring slightly. I remember the frantic hours spent crafting that analysis, the late nights fueled by lukewarm coffee, the sheer, exhausting effort I’d poured into those words. Now, they belonged to someone else. A stranger had taken the credit, the nuance, the very voice I had painstakingly developed. I stared at the screen, my fingers hovering over the delete button, a tremor running through my hands. A wave of hot shame washed over me, making my cheeks burn. How could I have been so careless? So easily overlooked? I felt a profound sense of inadequacy settle in, heavy and suffocating.

[story 2]

I found the link buried deep within a cluttered folder—a ghost of a notification from the journal. When I clicked it, the headline screamed my title, but the byline was utterly alien. It was someone else’s triumph. I leaned back in my worn office chair, the springs groaning in protest beneath me. My jaw tightened, and I pressed the heels of my hands against my eyes, trying to scrub the image away. Every carefully constructed sentence, every insightful observation, felt tainted, borrowed. A sharp, acidic taste flooded my mouth. I started pacing the small space, my movements jerky and agitated. "Impossible," I muttered to the empty room, the sound thin and brittle. The silence of the apartment amplified the internal noise—a relentless cataloging of every perceived failure, every missed opportunity that had led to this moment of stark realization.

[story 3]

The notification arrived on a Tuesday morning, utterly devoid of fanfare, just a sterile confirmation of publication. I opened the document, and the familiar structure of my argument was there, yet the signature belonged to a name I didn't recognize. A slow, sinking feeling began in my chest, spreading outward until my breath hitched. I picked up the printout, the paper feeling flimsy and disrespectful in my hands. I traced the unfamiliar name with a fingertip, a gesture heavy with accusation. A deep furrow settled between my brows, drawing the skin tight across my forehead. Why? What had I done to deserve this erasure? The realization wasn't just about the credit; it was a dismantling of everything I thought I was capable of producing. I dropped the paper onto the desk with a soft thud, the sound echoing the sudden collapse of my composure.
